Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'd try to set aside time on weekends mostly, but with limited resources at home and juggling extracurriculars, it never really stuck to a schedule. I'd usually end up cramming visual materials like diagrams and videos whenever I could find a quiet moment. The anxiety about keeping up made it hard to establish any real consistency.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due. I'd have weeks where I barely touched anything, then suddenly I'd be up late trying to catch up before a deadline. The stress of not having a regular routine actually made my anxiety worse, which created this vicious cycle where I'd avoid studying because it stressed me out, then stress more because I hadn't studied.
Q3: Usually it was panic about an upcoming deadline that got me to log in. Sometimes I'd get an email notification about something due and that would be my wake-up call. I rarely logged in just to browse or stay on top of things proactively - it was almost always reactive, like putting out fires.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. The LMS never became a natural part of my routine. I tried a few times to make it a habit, like checking it every Monday morning, but something would always come up with my extracurriculars or I'd just feel too bored and unmotivated to bother. It always felt like a chore rather than something integrated into my life.
Q5: The video content was definitely the most useful for me since I'm a visual learner. I watched over four hours of videos because I could actually absorb information that way better than reading dense text. Diagrams and visual explanations just clicked for me in ways that written materials didn't, especially when my motivation was already low.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the discussion forums most of the time. I only posted twice the whole course. They felt pointless to me - like busy work that wouldn't actually help me understand the material better. Reading other people's posts when I was already bored and anxious just felt like a waste of the limited energy I had for studying.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to the exam or assignments. If I could visualize how it would help my grade, I'd do it. Abstract discussions or optional readings that seemed disconnected from assessment just didn't register as important when I was already struggling to find motivation for the essential stuff.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford mentally. With my anxiety levels being what they were, I had to triage everything. Required work got done because it had to, but optional stuff felt like adding more weight to an already heavy load. There was always this guilt about not doing them, but never enough motivation to actually engage.
Q9: Yeah, a lot of my actual studying happened offline. I'd download lecture slides and videos when I had good internet access, then study them later on my own time. With limited resources, I couldn't always be online, so I'd screenshot diagrams, save PDFs, and review them repeatedly without the LMS ever knowing I was studying.
Q10: Most of my exam prep happened with printed notes and downloaded materials spread across my desk. I'd spend hours going over visual diagrams I'd saved, redrawing them to memorize concepts. None of that showed up anywhere in the system. My exam score was decent, but the LMS would have no idea how much offline work went into that.
Q11: Absolutely. I'd watch a video once on the LMS, then download it or take detailed visual notes and never watch it through the platform again. I'd review those notes maybe five or six times before an exam. The LMS only saw one view, but that material got way more attention than the data would suggest.
Q12: There were definitely low points, especially mid-semester when everything piled up. My anxiety would spike, I'd feel completely overwhelmed, and logging into the LMS felt like facing a mountain I couldn't climb. The boredom mixed with stress created this paralysis where I knew I should engage but just... couldn't bring myself to do it.
Q13: All of those things affected me constantly. Being low on resources meant I couldn't always access things when I needed to. Fatigue from extracurriculars ate into study time. When I was confused about material, the anxiety made it harder to seek help. It all created this perfect storm where LMS engagement just dropped off even when I knew I should be doing more.
Q14: Oh yeah, plenty of times. I'd have the LMS open, scrolling through materials, but my mind was completely elsewhere. The anxiety made it hard to focus even when I was technically 'engaged.' I'd read the same paragraph three times and retain nothing. Those sessions probably counted as activity but were basically useless learning-wise.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts constantly because they felt pointless and draining. I'd also avoid starting assignments until the last minute because the anxiety of potentially failing made it easier to just not think about them. It's irrational, I know - the CBT sessions helped me see that pattern - but knowing it didn't always stop it from happening.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. Without a deadline looming, I had almost no drive to log in or engage. Everything happened in these frantic bursts right before something was due. It wasn't healthy and it definitely contributed to my stress, but it was the only thing that could cut through the boredom and inertia.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. Good feedback gave me a temporary boost, but it faded quickly. Negative feedback would send my anxiety through the roof and sometimes made me avoid that type of work even more. I know feedback is supposed to help, but with my mental state, it often felt more like judgment than guidance.
Q18: The course structure felt pretty rigid, which didn't work great for someone like me with irregular access and low motivation. Having everything laid out in weekly modules meant I'd fall behind and then face this intimidating backlog. A more flexible structure might have helped me engage at my own pace without the constant feeling of being behind.
Q19: The video content being available for download made things easier - that was a lifesaver given my situation. But the emphasis on discussion participation felt forced and didn't match my learning style at all. The attendance requirements were also tough since I could only make about a fifth of the sessions, which added to my anxiety about falling behind.
Q20: I think that could be someone who's anxious and checking things repeatedly without actually processing them. Or maybe they're searching for something specific and can't find it, so they keep clicking around. High activity doesn't mean high understanding - sometimes it means confusion or anxiety driving repetitive, unproductive behavior.
Q21: That actually sounds a lot like me. I didn't log in much, but I studied a lot offline with downloaded materials. My exam score was over 80, which doesn't match my low engagement numbers at all. Some students just learn better outside the platform, or they're efficient with the time they do spend online and then work independently.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns - when someone logs in, what they click on, how long they spend. But they completely miss the quality of that engagement, offline studying, mental state, and real understanding. They see the shadow of learning, not learning itself. My logs would show a disengaged student, but my grades tell a different story.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and connecting with the material, not just going through the motions. It's when you're curious, when concepts click, when you want to learn more. For me, real engagement happened in quiet moments with my downloaded notes, not in my sparse LMS activity. It's internal, not measurable by clicks.
Q24: The LMS can't show my anxiety levels, my resource limitations, or the hours I spent studying offline. It can't capture the CBT sessions I did to try to manage my mental health around academics. It doesn't know about the visual diagrams I redrew dozens of times or the real effort behind my decent exam performance. The human side of learning is invisible to it.
